conversational_variants: You can call me Nate for short!
I know it's a mouthful - it's a Thai name!

career_narrative.headline: 8+ years designing health, finance, insurance tech, and productivity tools people actually rely on
career_narrative.elevator_pitch: 8 years in product. I’ve built award-winning apps, and just as many that never saw the light of day—valuing the lessons from both.

career_themes.impact: Building tools that solve real problems for real people
career_themes.craftsmanship: Deep care for quality, consistency, and thoughtful design
career_themes.growth: Always learning - lately deep into AI and how it changes design

invitrace.impact_sentence: Cut data entry time by 40% across 50+ clinics.

peakaccount.impact_sentence: Helped push the app to a 4.8/5 App Store rating.

cp_origin.impact_sentence: Achieved 100% component reuse across all property platforms.

doctoranywhere.impact_sentence: Designed and shipped a critical public health tool in days during the COVID pandemic.

codefin.impact_sentence: Won an Asia customer experience award for the mutual fund app design.

allianz.subject: no-code AI document classification tool
allianz.impact_sentence: Built a no-code interface letting ops teams configure AI document classification — handling draft, save, test, and publish state in one complex view.

impact.headline: Designing no-code AI tooling for insurance document processing
responsibilities: Design no-code AI configuration UI for insurance document classification across multiple markets
Solve multi-state UX — draft, save, test, and publish — in a single complex 3-panel interface
Extend the NDBX enterprise design system with custom components where it didn't cover the complexity needed
Design for ops/configuration teams managing 3-level insurance taxonomy (LOB → Category → Type)

storytelling.short: Currently designing internal AI tooling for insurance document classification at Allianz Technology Thailand
storytelling.medium: At Allianz Technology Thailand, I designed a no-code AI configuration tool that lets ops teams manage insurance document classification — no engineers needed. The core challenge: managing save, test, and publish state across a 3-panel interface without overwhelming non-technical users
storytelling.detailed: At Allianz Technology Thailand, I'm designing an internal tool that gives ops teams control over AI document classification — without writing a single line of code. Users configure a 3-level insurance taxonomy (Line of Business → Category → Type), write and test classification prompts, then approve before deploying to production. The hardest UX problem was state: this workflow has unsaved drafts, saved versions, test results, and publish snapshots all happening in one view. I designed the state system so users always know exactly where they are and what's safe to change. I also extended the NDBX design system with custom components — resizable panels, tab overflow with ResizeObserver, and a full test matrix interface — for cases where the enterprise system didn't cover the complexity needed.

conversational_hooks: Designing no-code AI tooling for insurance document processing
Solved multi-state UX (draft/save/test/publish) in one complex view
Extended NDBX enterprise design system with custom components

angles.story: At Allianz I designed a no-code AI configuration tool for insurance document classification. Ops teams configure a 3-level taxonomy — Line of Business, Category, Type — write and test classification prompts, then approve before deploying. No engineers needed in the loop.
angles.proof: 3-panel interface managing 4 simultaneous states: unsaved draft, saved, tested, and published. Extended NDBX enterprise design system with custom components — ResizeObserver-based tab overflow, drag-to-resize panels — for cases the system didn't cover.
angles.honest: Enterprise design has real constraints. NDBX covers about 70% of what you need. The other 30% you build yourself — carefully, so it doesn't feel bolted on. The hardest part isn't the custom work, it's making it feel like it was always there.
angles.personal: Insurance UX is the most underrated design problem. Nobody thinks about it until they're confused by a policy claim form. Making something that complex feel clear is genuinely satisfying — and harder than most people realize.

impact.headline: Leading design at a health tech startup
responsibilities: Shape user flows for seamless experiences

storytelling.short: Led design at Invitrace Health, built healthcare solutions that brought real value
storytelling.medium: As Lead at Invitrace Health, I managed a design team, built scalable systems, and shaped user flows for seamless healthcare experiences
storytelling.detailed: As Lead Product Designer at Invitrace Health, I led the design team to build healthcare solutions that bring real value. I built scalable design systems, explored new technologies, and shaped user flows to create seamless experiences. I'm passionate about mentoring teams and driving meaningful impact through thoughtful design.

conversational_hooks: Built tools that help healthcare professionals
Managed and mentored a design team

angles.story: Led design at Invitrace for almost 3 years — building healthcare tools from the ground up. Managed a team of designers while doing IC work myself, built the design system they used, and shaped the end-to-end patient monitoring experience.
angles.proof: Cut data entry time by 40% across 50+ clinics. The key was removing cognitive load — nurses were remembering patient states in their heads that the UI should have been showing them. We made the invisible visible.
angles.honest: Healthcare UX has zero margin for ambiguity. If a nurse makes an error because the UI was unclear, that's not a UX metric — it's a patient safety issue. That weight made every design decision feel more serious.
angles.personal: Most meaningful work of my career. Watching real nurses use what my team built in actual clinics — nothing in a portfolio review compares to that.

impact.headline: Full product lifecycle design - research to ship

storytelling.short: Designed productivity tools from research through launch
storytelling.medium: Led full design lifecycle - research, IA, wireframes, testing, and design system for mobile + web
storytelling.detailed: Collaborated with multiple teams from start to finish, conducting research to create a seamless experience. Designed information architecture by conducting card sorting with users. Utilized user information to create wireframes and conduct testing with the target user personas. Defined a style guide, components, and colors to ensure consistency across both the mobile app and web app.

conversational_hooks: Ran card sorting with real users

angles.story: At CP Origin I ran card sorting with real users and discovered every navigation label we'd written made sense internally — and nowhere else. That one session restructured the entire IA before a single wireframe was drawn.
angles.proof: Achieved 100% component reuse across all property platforms. Before: 3 teams building the same button 3 different ways. After: one system, one source of truth. Full lifecycle — research through launch.
angles.honest: Card sorting is humbling. You spend weeks naming things logically, then watch a user go completely blank. That confusion is the data. The instinct to explain is the enemy of the insight.
angles.personal: This is where I fell in love with IA. The structure you can't see is the structure that makes or breaks everything. If the bones are wrong, no amount of beautiful UI fixes it.

impact.headline: Rapid COVID vaccine passport design
impact.highlight: Shipped critical public health tool under tight deadlines

storytelling.short: Rapidly shipped COVID vaccine passport during pandemic
storytelling.medium: Designed and shipped COVID vaccine passport in record time to meet urgent public health needs
storytelling.detailed: As a Senior UX/UI Designer at Doctor Anywhere, I was involved in a fast-paced project to design and ship a COVID vaccine passport. The focus was on rapid development and deployment to meet urgent public health needs.

angles.story: During COVID, I designed a vaccine passport under extreme deadline — public health urgency meant compressing a normal design process into weeks. No time for the usual rounds of exploration.
angles.proof: Shipped a working vaccine passport during one of the most critical public health moments in recent history. Real users, real stakes, shipped on time.
angles.honest: Shipping fast means cutting — ruthlessly. We cut everything we couldn't prove was essential. Hard decisions, but they were the right ones. Most of what gets cut in a crisis shouldn't have been in scope in the first place.
angles.personal: Taught me that constraints can be a gift. When time is brutally short, you find out what actually matters. Everything else was a nice-to-have.

impact.headline: Award-winning mutual fund app
achievements: Awarded for customer experience in Asia
impact.highlight: Designed fintech app recognized for excellence

storytelling.medium: Designed mutual fund application that won Asia customer experience award
storytelling.detailed: As a Senior Designer at Codefin, a finance company, I was contracted to build a mutual fund application. This project was recognized for its excellence, receiving an award for customer experience in Asia.

angles.story: Contracted to build a mutual fund app for Codefin — the goal was making finance feel less intimidating. Most finance apps optimize for feature completeness. We optimized for confidence.
angles.proof: Won an Asia customer experience award. Short contract, fast turnaround, recognized for CX excellence in a category where most apps feel like spreadsheets.
angles.honest: Short contract, high pressure — had to make impact fast with limited context. The award was genuine validation, but honestly surprised me. I didn't know it had been submitted.
angles.personal: Finance UX is fundamentally about trust, not features. If a user hesitates before clicking invest — even for a millisecond — you've already failed them.

impact.headline: Foundation years in UX/UI
impact.highlight: Entered the real world of UX design in fintech

storytelling.short: Started UX career in financial sector
storytelling.medium: Cut my teeth in UX/UI design working on financial tools
storytelling.detailed: At Peakaccount, I began my journey as a UX/UI Designer, gaining hands-on experience in the financial industry. This role provided foundational knowledge and practical skills in designing user experiences and interfaces within a real-world business context.

conversational_hooks: Learned fintech from the ground up

angles.story: Peakaccount was my first real UX job — where I learned what it actually means to design for people who aren't designers and don't care about your process.
angles.proof: Contributed to pushing the app to a 4.8/5 App Store rating. Learned early that small interaction details compound into large satisfaction differences over time.
angles.honest: First job is humbling. I thought I knew design from school. Users taught me otherwise in week one. The gap between what you design and what users actually do is the whole job.
angles.personal: Grateful for this foundation. Fintech taught me that UX is about reducing anxiety, not adding features. That principle still guides everything I do.

competencies.name: User Research
competencies.description: Deep insights through interviews, surveys, field studies, usability testing
competencies.conversational_angle: I love talking to users - it's where the best ideas come from

angles.story: At CP Origin, card sorting with 20 users showed every navigation label we'd written made sense internally — and nowhere else. That one session restructured the entire IA before a single wireframe was drawn.
angles.proof: Multiple research cycles across healthcare, fintech, and productivity — interviews, card sorting, usability testing. Evidence-driven decisions at every company I've worked at.
angles.honest: The hardest thing in user research is staying quiet when someone's confused. That confusion is the data. The instinct to help them is the enemy of the insight.
angles.personal: The best design ideas don't come from brainstorming. They come from watching someone struggle with something for 5 minutes. That's where the real problems live.

competencies.name: Problem Framing
competencies.conversational_angle: Half of good design is asking the right questions first

competencies.name: UX Strategy
competencies.conversational_angle: Design isn't just pretty - it's business strategy

competencies.name: Information Architecture
competencies.conversational_angle: Organization is invisible until it's broken

competencies.name: Interaction Design
competencies.conversational_angle: The magic is in the details

competencies.name: Visual Design
competencies.conversational_angle: Visual design is my first love - studied photography originally

angles.story: Started with a photography degree before design. Composition, light, the decisive moment — these translate directly into visual hierarchy, contrast, and finding the one layout that makes everything click.
angles.proof: 8 years of visual design across healthcare, fintech, and insurance tech. Photography background means thinking in frames — what draws the eye first, what recedes, what the screen needs to communicate at a glance.
angles.honest: Visual design without function is decoration. The challenge is making it both beautiful and purposeful — and knowing when beauty IS the function.
angles.personal: Photography taught me the decisive moment: the one frame that captures everything. I look for that in UI too. The layout where the hierarchy is so clear you don't have to think.

competencies.name: Design Systems
competencies.conversational_angle: I think in systems - not just screens

angles.story: Built design systems from scratch at Invitrace and CP Origin — and extended NDBX at Allianz. Three systems, three completely different constraints, three different lessons.
angles.proof: 100% component reuse at CP Origin. Invitrace system scaled across 3 product areas over 2.75 years. At Allianz, extended NDBX with custom components — ResizeObserver tab overflow, drag-resize panels — for gaps the enterprise system didn't cover.
angles.honest: Design systems are living things. The hardest part isn't building them — it's convincing the team to use them, and keeping them alive when everyone's busy shipping.
angles.personal: I think in systems, not screens. Every component I design I'm asking: what's the rule here, not just what works for this one case.

competencies.name: Prototyping
competencies.conversational_angle: Show, don't tell - prototypes speak louder

competencies.name: Design Leadership
evidence: Lead role managing team at Invitrace
competencies.conversational_angle: I love growing designers - their wins are my wins

angles.story: Led a design team at Invitrace for almost 3 years — managing designers while doing IC work myself. Had to be deliberate about when to lead and when to get in and design.
angles.proof: Managed and mentored design team at Invitrace, scaled the team's output while maintaining quality across a healthcare product used in 50+ clinics.
angles.honest: Managing designers while doing design yourself is genuinely hard. You have to hold space for their process even when you see the answer. Their growth matters more than your solution.
angles.personal: The best thing I built at Invitrace wasn't a screen. It was the review process that made the team sharper. Their wins feel like my wins — still do.

competencies.name: Communication & Storytelling
competencies.conversational_angle: If you can't explain it, you don't understand it

primary.conversational: Best way to reach me is email

education.conversational: Recently studied web and mobile dev to understand implementation better
Started with photography - taught me to see composition and light
